CLARITY.justification: Overall the paper is well organized (Intro → Related Work → Method → Experiments → Discussion) and clearly states its key contribution early (intro: layerwise optimization for PL‑CNNs and casting layer updates as DC/latent SVM problems). Notation is mostly consistent and definitions (PL functions, W^l, Φ, H, Ψ) are provided and used coherently (see Proposition 1–3 and Algorithm 1). Presentation issues are minor but present: several in-text references to figures/tables are not accompanied by the visuals in the excerpt, and there are sections with repeated/garbled sentences and formatting glitches (long duplicated paragraph block near the end) that reduce readability. Overall clear and technically well-presented but would benefit from proofreading and ensuring figures/tables are properly integrated.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper presents a notable and (to my reading) genuinely new perspective: the key observation that per-layer parameter estimation in PL-CNNs is a difference-of-convex program equivalent to a latent structured SVM (Proposition 2) and thus can be optimized via CCCP with structured-SVM solvers (Proposition 3 and Algorithm 1). While the building blocks (CCCP, latent SVMs, BCFW) are existing techniques, the paper’s identification of this connection and the practical extensions (trust-region initialization, compact feature representation, constraint-reduction in Section 4.3) that make the approach scalable (ImageNet experiments) constitute a non-trivial and well-differentiated contribution relative to cited prior work (ADMM, convex relaxations, second-order methods). Hence the work is notably novel though it composes known methods rather than introducing an entirely new algorithmic primitive.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The core technical claims are plausibly and coherently supported: Proposition 1 (composition of PL functions) and the DC/latent-SVM reformulation (Prop.2) are justified and accompanied by constructive decompositions (Appendix A–B), and the dual/BCFW derivation with the proximal warm-start (Appendix D) is sound. The algorithmic reductions (feature-vector memory trick, solving latent argmax via forward/back passes) are analytically correct (chain-rule, activation selection) and the experiments show consistent empirical gains. Minor concerns remain: several proofs are sketched rather than fully detailed, the practical reduction of the latent search space (Y vs Y×H) is empirical and may not hold generally, and the experimental protocol fine-tunes from SGD solutions (no statistical tests, limited timing/compute comparisons), so some claims about broad applicability and "no learning-rate tuning" are slightly overstated but do not invalidate the main results.
